Premiums are usually paid by the employer (or shared). No medical underwriting for individual employees — all are covered regardless of pre-existing conditions, usually from day one. Coverage ends when employment terminates. Benefit: covers PEDs from day one. Risk: no continuity if you leave the job.
example: A tech company provides group health cover of ₹5 lakh to all employees and their families. An employee with diabetes is covered from Day 1 — no waiting period. Upon resignation, coverage ends. The ex-employee must buy an individual policy, and the PED 30-day moratorium or standard waiting period applies fresh.

definition: A 14-digit unique health ID issued under the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM). Links all health records (prescriptions, lab reports, hospital discharge summaries, insurance claims) in a unified digital health profile. Created using Aadhaar or mobile number. Enables paperless hospital admissions, claim filing, and health record access. Increasingly integrated with insurance TPAs for faster cashless processing.
example: A patient with an ABHA number at a participating hospital can share their health records digitally with the TPA for cashless pre-authorization — eliminating the need for physical document submission. Previous hospitalization records are instantly accessible to doctors.

definition: A benefit paid to the policyholder if they survive a specified period (survival benefit) or reach a specified age under a money-back or endowment-type policy. Unlike death benefit (paid to nominee on death), survivorship benefits are paid to the living policyholder at defined milestones. Common in money-back plans where 20–25% of SA is paid every 5 years during the policy term.
example: A 20-year money-back policy with ₹10 lakh SA: 20% paid at year 5 (₹2 lakh), 20% at year 10 (₹2 lakh), 20% at year 15 (₹2 lakh), and 40% + bonuses at maturity (year 20). These payments are survivorship/survival benefits.
source: LIC / Private Life Insurer Product Terms

definition: The period (typically the first 2–3 years of a policy) during which the insurer can investigate and potentially deny a claim due to misrepresentation, fraud, or material non-disclosure at the time of application. After this period (governed by Section 45 of the Insurance Act), the insurer's right to contest is severely limited. Also called the 'incontestability period' in reverse — once it lapses, the policy becomes incontestable.
example: A 40-year-old buys a term policy concealing a heart condition diagnosed 1 year ago. Dies in year 2 — insurer investigates and can reject the claim due to non-disclosure (within contestability period). Dies in year 4 — insurer generally CANNOT reject the claim (Section 45 protection applies).

definition: A fundamental principle of insurance — the policyholder must have a legitimate financial interest in the insured person or property. Without insurable interest, the insurance contract is void. For life insurance: self (unlimited), spouse, children, and business partners (to the extent of financial interest). For property insurance: owners, mortgagees, lessees have insurable interest.
example: A wife has insurable interest in her husband's life. An employee does NOT have insurable interest in a stranger's life. A business partner has insurable interest in a co-partner's life up to the financial loss they'd suffer. Buying life insurance without insurable interest is wagering (prohibited).

definition: The current market cost of rebuilding or replacing an asset to its original condition using new materials. Relevant in property insurance (building/home insurance). Different from IDV (which applies depreciation). A 'reinstatement value policy' or 'new for old policy' pays the full replacement cost without depreciation. Common for commercial property insurance in India.
example: A factory building has an IDV of ₹2 crore (market value after depreciation) but a reinstatement value of ₹3.5 crore (cost to rebuild at current construction rates). A reinstatement value policy would pay ₹3.5 crore to rebuild after a fire — an IDV-based policy would pay only ₹2 crore.

definition: A penalty fee deducted when a ULIP or investment-linked insurance policy is surrendered before the lock-in period (typically 5 years) or a specified holding period. IRDAI regulations cap ULIP surrender charges — from year 1 to 5, maximum charges are defined. After the lock-in period, surrender charges drop to zero in most ULIPs. Traditional policies may also have implicit surrender charges via lower Guaranteed Surrender Value in early years.
example: If a ULIP is surrendered in year 2, IRDAI caps the surrender charge — the insurer cannot charge more than a specified percentage of premium or fund value. After 5 years (lock-in), most ULIPs have zero surrender charges — the full fund value is paid on surrender.

definition: The minimum period for which a ULIP policyholder must stay invested before they can withdraw funds or surrender the policy. For ULIPs, IRDAI mandates a 5-year lock-in period. During lock-in, if the policy is surrendered, the surrender value is credited to a discontinued policy fund and paid only after the 5-year lock-in expires, with minimal returns (4% p.a. as mandated). After lock-in, partial withdrawals are permitted.
example: A ULIP started in 2020, lock-in ends in 2025. If surrendered in 2022, the fund value (minus surrender charges) goes into a discontinued policy fund earning 4% p.a. At 2025, the accumulated amount is paid. If surrendered after 2025, the full fund value is paid immediately.

definition: The facility in ULIPs that allows policyholders to withdraw a portion of the fund value after the 5-year lock-in period, without surrendering the policy. The policy continues after partial withdrawal. IRDAI limits: partial withdrawals are permitted up to 20% of fund value per year after lock-in. Withdrawals are tax-free under Section 10(10D) if premiums paid are less than 10% of sum assured.
example: A ULIP started in 2018, fund value in 2025 = ₹15 lakh. After lock-in (5 years from 2018 = 2023), the policyholder can withdraw up to ₹3 lakh (20% of ₹15 lakh) while keeping the policy active. Remaining ₹12 lakh stays invested.

definition: A ULIP feature allowing policyholders to move their accumulated corpus between different investment funds (equity, debt, balanced, liquid) within the same policy based on risk appetite and market conditions. Most ULIPs offer 4–12 free switches per year; additional switches attract nominal charges (₹100–₹500). Switching does not trigger capital gains tax — it is a tax-neutral operation within the ULIP wrapper.
example: A ULIP policyholder moves ₹5 lakh from an equity fund (70% stocks) to a balanced fund (50:50) when markets are near a peak — this is a fund switch. No tax is payable on this transfer. The move rebalances portfolio risk without triggering LTCG.

definition: A facility within ULIPs allowing automatic, periodic transfer of funds between investment funds — similar to SIP in mutual funds. Used to gradually move corpus from a liquid/debt fund to equity (for rupee-cost averaging) or from equity to debt (for systematic de-risking near maturity). Reduces timing risk of lump-sum fund switches.
example: A ULIP policyholder with ₹10 lakh in a liquid fund sets up an STP to transfer ₹1 lakh/month into an equity fund over 10 months. This achieves cost averaging rather than making a single large switch at a potentially wrong market timing.

definition: The application form submitted to an insurer when buying insurance — it is the basis of the insurance contract. The policyholder must disclose all material facts truthfully: health history, lifestyle habits, occupation, income, existing policies. Any misrepresentation or non-disclosure in the proposal form can give the insurer grounds to repudiate claims (especially within the contestability period). The proposal form becomes part of the policy document.
example: A proposal form for a term plan asks: 'Have you been diagnosed with diabetes in the last 5 years?' If the applicant answers 'No' falsely, this is material misrepresentation. If they die within 3 years, the insurer can investigate and reject the claim.

definition: The failure to disclose information that would have influenced an insurer's decision to accept, reject, or price the insurance policy. If material non-disclosure is proven, the insurer can repudiate the claim or void the policy (within the contestability period). Common examples: undisclosed pre-existing diseases, hazardous occupation, previous policy rejections, tobacco/alcohol use.
example: A person buys health insurance without disclosing they were diagnosed with hypertension 2 years ago. This is material non-disclosure. If they are hospitalized for a hypertension complication, the insurer can reject the claim citing non-disclosure.
source: Insurance Act 1938 / Common Law Principle of Utmost Good Faith

definition: The foundational legal principle of insurance contracts requiring both parties (insurer and insured) to act in complete honesty and disclose all material facts relevant to the insurance contract. The insured must disclose all material facts; the insurer must clearly explain policy terms and exclusions. Failure of utmost good faith by either party can void the contract.
example: When buying life insurance, Anand must disclose his smoking habit (10 cigarettes/day) even if the proposal form does not explicitly ask — because it is a material fact affecting life expectancy. Not disclosing this is a breach of utmost good faith.
source: Marine Insurance Act 1906 (applied to all insurance) / Insurance Act 1938

definition: The right of a health insurance policyholder to shift from one insurer to another without losing credit for waiting periods (PED, specific disease waiting periods) served with the previous insurer. IRDAI mandates portability at renewal. The new insurer must accept the portability application and cannot charge extra premium solely due to portability. Portability applies to the same or lower sum insured — higher SI upgrades may require fresh underwriting.
example: A customer with Bajaj Allianz health insurance for 4 years (with a 4-year PED waiting period for diabetes served) switches to Star Health at renewal. Star Health must credit 4 years of PED waiting period — so diabetes is already covered from day 1 with Star Health.

definition: An add-on rider to a life or health policy that pays a lump sum on diagnosis of specified critical illnesses (typically 10–40 illnesses including cancer, heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, major organ transplant). The payout is made irrespective of medical expenses — it is a defined benefit. The base policy continues after the CI payout. IRDAI standardizes definitions of 9 'major critical illnesses' for uniformity.
example: A ₹50 lakh term plan with a ₹25 lakh CI rider. On diagnosis of major cancer (as per IRDAI definition), the insurer pays ₹25 lakh immediately. The policyholder can use this for treatment, income replacement, or debt repayment. The ₹50 lakh death benefit (minus CI amount) remains active.
source: IRDAI Rider Guidelines / Standard CI Definitions

definition: Optional add-on benefits attached to a base insurance policy that enhance coverage for additional premium. Common riders: Accidental Death Benefit (ADB), Waiver of Premium (WOP), Critical Illness (CI), Income Benefit, Hospital Cash, Permanent Disability. Riders must be attached at policy inception or at specific renewal milestones. IRDAI rules: rider premium cannot exceed 30% of base policy premium; rider benefit cannot exceed base policy benefit.
example: A term plan with base premium of ₹15,000/year can have riders costing up to ₹4,500/year (30% of ₹15,000). Common rider combination: ₹1,500 (ADB rider) + ₹1,200 (WOP rider) + ₹1,800 (CI rider) = ₹4,500 total rider premium.

definition: Actuarial reserve that an insurer sets aside for claims that have been incurred (treatment has happened) but not yet reported to the insurer. Important in health and motor insurance where there is a lag between loss occurrence and claim filing. IBNR reserving ensures the insurer has sufficient funds when delayed claims arrive.
example: In December 2024, a policyholder is hospitalized for surgery but submits the claim in February 2025. The insurer maintains an IBNR reserve for claims like this that occurred in December but will be reported in the future. This is standard actuarial practice.

definition: An additional percentage added to the base premium due to higher-than-standard risk factors in the applicant's profile. Insurers may load premiums for: history of pre-existing illness, higher BMI, family history of hereditary diseases, hazardous occupation, or high claims history. In health insurance, IRDAI permits loading at renewal if the policyholder's claims experience is significantly worse than the pool average.
example: A 45-year-old applicant with obesity (BMI > 35) and family history of heart disease may be offered a standard health policy with a 20% loading — meaning the premium is 20% higher than the standard rate for a 45-year-old.

definition: The process by which an insurer assesses the risk profile of an applicant and decides whether to accept, reject, or modify the insurance offer (via loading, exclusion, or reduced cover). For life insurance: health underwriting (medical reports, lifestyle disclosures) and financial underwriting (income verification for large SA). For health insurance: health declaration and medical tests for older applicants. Group policies use 'experience underwriting' (based on group's past claims).
example: A 55-year-old applying for a ₹1 crore term plan must undergo medical tests (ECG, blood glucose, lipid profile). If reports show diabetes, the insurer may: accept with standard premium, add 'diabetes loading' (20% extra premium), or exclude diabetes complications from rider benefits.

definition: An insurance policy covering financial losses during domestic or international travel. Key coverages: emergency medical expenses abroad, trip cancellation, trip interruption, baggage loss or delay, flight cancellation, personal liability, and repatriation of remains. Premiums are based on destination, trip duration, traveler's age, and sum insured. Student travel plans and senior citizen travel plans are specialized variants.
example: A person traveling to the USA for 15 days buys a travel policy with $1,00,000 medical cover, $1,000 baggage loss, $2,000 trip cancellation, and $500 flight delay cover. If they fall sick abroad and hospitalized for 3 days ($15,000 bill), the insurer pays the hospital directly or reimburses.

definition: A travel insurance benefit that reimburses non-refundable travel expenses (flights, hotels, tour packages) if the trip is cancelled due to a covered reason BEFORE departure. Covered reasons: serious illness or death of traveler or immediate family member, natural disaster at destination, terrorist attack, visa denial (some policies). Covered amount: typically the pre-paid, non-refundable trip cost.
example: Priya books a ₹2 lakh Europe trip (flights + hotel, non-refundable). She falls seriously ill 3 days before departure. Her travel insurance's trip cancellation cover reimburses ₹2 lakh — provided the illness is certified by a doctor as preventing travel.

definition: A travel insurance benefit that reimburses emergency purchase of essential items (clothing, toiletries, medicines) if checked-in baggage is delayed beyond a specified period (typically 6–12 hours) by the airline. This is different from baggage loss cover (which applies when baggage is permanently lost). Compensation is usually a fixed amount per hour of delay (e.g., ₹500/hour up to ₹5,000).
example: Flights into London, but checked-in baggage is delayed for 18 hours. The traveler buys toiletries (₹2,000) and essential clothing (₹5,000) while waiting. Travel policy with baggage delay cover reimburses up to the policy sub-limit — say ₹6,000.

definition: Standalone insurance covering bodily injury resulting from an accident. Covers: accidental death (AD), permanent total disability (PTD), permanent partial disability (PPD), and temporary total disability (TTD). Benefit is paid as a lump sum (for AD, PTD, PPD) or weekly compensation (for TTD). PA insurance does NOT cover illness — only accidents. Highly affordable: ₹10 lakh cover typically costs ₹100–₹200/year for a 30-year-old.
example: A construction worker falls from a scaffold, loses use of both legs permanently (PTD). His PA policy of ₹25 lakh pays ₹25 lakh (100% of SA for PTD). If only one arm is lost (PPD — say 50% disability), the policy pays 50% of SA = ₹12.5 lakh.

definition: A disability classification in PA insurance where the insured is permanently and completely unable to perform any occupation or gainful employment due to an accidental injury. Examples: loss of both eyes, loss of both hands, loss of both legs, or any combination that renders the person completely unable to work. PTD benefit is typically 100% of the PA sum assured paid as a lump sum.
example: A delivery driver loses both hands in a factory accident. Medical board certifies permanent total disability. His PA policy (₹20 lakh SA) pays ₹20 lakh (100% of SA) as a lump sum on PTD certification. He can also apply for ESIC disability pension if employed.

definition: A disability classification in PA insurance where the insured loses partial use or function of a body part permanently due to an accident. Each body part has a defined compensation percentage of the sum assured (e.g., loss of one eye = 50%, loss of one hand = 50%, loss of one finger = 10%). These percentages are standardized by IRDAI in the standard PA policy.
example: A road accident causes permanent loss of sight in one eye. PA policy with ₹15 lakh SA: one eye = 50% disability percentage → payout = ₹7.5 lakh. If the accident had caused loss of sight in BOTH eyes, it would be PTD (100% = ₹15 lakh).
source: IRDAI Standard PA Policy Schedule of Disabilities

definition: A temporary disability from an accident that prevents the insured from working for a period of time — but is not permanent. TTD benefit in PA insurance is typically a weekly compensation (e.g., ₹5,000/week or 1% of SA per week) for the duration of the disability, subject to a maximum of 104 weeks. Requires medical certificate confirming disability and period of rest.
example: A software engineer breaks both wrists in a cycle accident and cannot type for 8 weeks. PA policy with TTD benefit of ₹5,000/week pays ₹40,000 total (8 weeks × ₹5,000) as income replacement during recovery.

definition: A health insurance benefit (standalone policy or rider) that pays a fixed daily cash amount for each day of hospitalization — regardless of actual medical expenses. Used to cover incidental expenses (food, transport for family, loss of income) not covered by standard health plans. Typical amount: ₹500–₹5,000/day. Waiting periods apply (usually 24 hours for the first payout). Does not require submission of medical bills — only proof of hospitalization.
example: A self-employed person with a hospital cash policy of ₹2,000/day is hospitalized for 7 days. They receive ₹14,000 flat — no bills needed, just a discharge summary. This compensates for lost business income during the hospital stay.

definition: An IRDAI-licensed intermediary that manages health insurance claims on behalf of insurers. Functions: issue health cards, maintain hospital networks, process pre-authorization for cashless claims, adjudicate and settle reimbursement claims, handle member queries. TPA receives a service fee from the insurer. Insurers can switch TPAs; some large insurers (Star Health, Care Health) manage claims in-house (in-house TPA). TPA is accountable to both the insurer and the policyholder.
example: HDFC ERGO uses Medi Assist TPA. When an HDFC ERGO policyholder goes to a network hospital, the hospital's insurance desk calls Medi Assist for pre-authorization, not HDFC ERGO directly. Medi Assist processes the cashless request and settles the hospital bill.

definition: A specific waiting period (typically 1–2 years) for certain named diseases or conditions, even in the absence of a pre-existing disease. Common diseases on the specific disease waiting period list: hernia, cataract, kidney stones, joint replacement, hemorrhoids, sinusitis, and hydrocele. During this period, treatment for these conditions is excluded even if the policyholder never had them before.
example: A new health policy with a 1-year specific disease waiting period for hernia: if the insured develops a hernia in month 8 and requires surgery, the claim is rejected. After month 12, the surgery is covered. This is different from PED — the person didn't have hernia before buying the policy.

definition: A standardized health insurance product mandated by IRDAI in 2020, with uniform features across all insurers. Key features: sum insured ₹1 lakh to ₹5 lakh, covers hospitalization, ICU, AYUSH, day care procedures, cataract up to ₹40,000, modern treatments up to 50% SI. 5% room rent sub-limit. Compulsory standard exclusions. Insurers cannot change core features. Enables easy comparison across insurers. Also called 'Arogya Sanjeevani Policy.'
example: Whether you buy 'Arogya Sanjeevani Policy' from LIC Health Insurance, Star Health, or HDFC ERGO — the core features are identical. Easy comparison means only price and service quality differ. Perfect for first-time buyers or senior citizens wanting simple, standardized cover.
source: IRDAI Circular on Standard Health Product 2020

definition: Insurance for insurers — a practice where primary insurers transfer a portion of their risk to reinsurance companies to reduce the financial impact of large claims. In India, GIC Re (General Insurance Corporation of India) is the national reinsurer mandated to receive the first right of refusal for all reinsurance treaties. Reinsurance enables insurers to offer large sum insureds (e.g., ₹1 crore+ health plans) without taking on full risk.
example: A life insurer sells a ₹5 crore term plan. To manage the risk, it reinsures ₹4 crore with GIC Re or a foreign reinsurer (Swiss Re, Munich Re). If the policyholder dies, the primary insurer pays ₹5 crore to the family — the reinsurer then pays the primary insurer its ₹4 crore share.

definition: A specialist professional who assesses financial risk using mathematics, statistics, and probability. In insurance, actuaries: design insurance products, calculate premiums based on mortality/morbidity tables, determine policy reserves, price riders, and certify solvency margins. Every insurer must have a Designated Actuary (DA) certified by the Institute of Actuaries of India (IAI). Premium pricing is ultimately signed off by the actuary.
example: The premium for a ₹1 crore term plan for a 35-year-old non-smoker male is ₹12,000/year. This is calculated by actuaries using: mortality rates for Indian males age 35, lapse rates, investment return assumptions, expense loadings, and profit margins — all compliant with IRDAI mandated mortality tables.
source: Insurance Act 1938 / IRDAI Appointed Actuary Regulations

definition: A financial health indicator of an insurance company — the ratio of available solvency margin (ASM) to required solvency margin (RSM). IRDAI mandates a minimum solvency ratio of 1.5 for all Indian insurers. Higher ratio = more financially stable insurer, less risk of default on claim payments. Typically disclosed in annual reports and IRDAI websites.
example: An insurer with a solvency ratio of 3.0 has twice the required financial cushion — very safe. An insurer with solvency ratio of 1.5 meets the minimum — acceptable but not a buffer. If an insurer's solvency ratio falls below 1.5, IRDAI can intervene and restrict new business.

definition: The cash amount an insurer pays when a traditional life insurance policy is terminated before maturity at the policyholder's request. Two components: Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV — minimum guaranteed by law under Insurance Act, based on premiums paid) and Special Surrender Value (SSV — higher value offered by insurer based on actual performance, typically used by LIC). Insurers pay the higher of GSV or SSV. Surrender value is available only after a minimum number of premiums are paid (typically 3 years for traditional plans).
example: LIC Jeevan Anand policy, premiums paid for 10 years, SA = ₹10 lakh: GSV = 30% of premiums paid (guaranteed minimum), SSV = ₹4.5 lakh (based on LIC's calculation). LIC pays SSV (₹4.5 lakh) as it is higher than GSV. Surrendering forfeits all future benefits including death cover.
source: Insurance Act 1938, Section 113 / LIC Policy Terms

definition: Low-premium, low-coverage insurance products designed for economically vulnerable populations in India. IRDAI's micro insurance regulations allow simplified products with sum assureds as low as ₹50,000 (life) or ₹10,000 (health). Simpler KYC, simpler underwriting, lower premiums. Distributed through NGOs, SHGs, MFIs, and Bima Vahaks to reach the uninsured rural population.
example: A village woman enrolled in a micro insurance scheme through her SHG pays ₹50/month for a health micro insurance policy with ₹50,000 hospitalization cover. If hospitalized, she receives reimbursement up to ₹50,000 — covering basic rural hospital costs.

definition: After an insurer pays a claim, subrogation gives the insurer the right to pursue the responsible third party to recover the paid claim amount. Common in motor insurance: if another party caused the accident, the insurer (after paying the policyholder's OD claim) can recover from the at-fault party's insurer. The policyholder must cooperate with the insurer's subrogation proceedings.
example: A truck hits and damages a car (policyholder's own car). The car insurer pays the own damage claim of ₹3 lakh. The insurer then subrogate against the truck owner's third-party insurer to recover ₹3 lakh. The car owner does not need to pursue the truck owner — the insurer does it.

definition: A fundamental principle of insurance claims — the insurer is liable only for losses proximately (directly) caused by a covered peril. If there is a chain of events, the proximate cause is the most immediate and dominant cause that set the events in motion. Remote or secondary causes that are excluded perils do not give rise to a claim even if they contribute to the loss.
example: A person has a heart attack while driving, loses control, and the car crashes. The proximate cause of vehicle damage is the accident (covered under OD). If the heart attack itself is claimed under health insurance, the hospitalization is covered. Motor claim is not denied just because the heart attack was the underlying trigger.

definition: IRDAI's annual publication reporting on the state of the insurance industry in India — including premium data, claim settlement ratios, solvency positions, consumer complaints, number of insurers, penetration rates, and regulatory actions. Published every year and available on irdai.gov.in. Key reference for comparing insurer performance metrics.
example: IRDAI Annual Report 2023-24 shows health insurance premium grew 20% YoY to ₹1.1 lakh crore. LIC's claim settlement ratio: 98.66%. Max Life: 99.51%. The report allows consumers to compare insurers' claim settlement performance.

definition: A growing feature in Indian health insurance where insurers reward policyholders for healthy behaviors tracked via wearables (fitness bands, smartwatches). Rewards may include: premium discounts at renewal, additional sum insured, shopping vouchers, or loyalty points. Used by Niva Bupa (ReAssure), Star Health (Star Wellness), and Aditya Birla Health Insurance (Activ program) to incentivize preventive health.
example: Niva Bupa's ReAssure 2.0 offers a 'Health Returns' feature: if the policyholder walks 10,000 steps/day and completes health check-ups, they earn health returns that can be used to offset claim amounts or premiums. Annual health return up to 30% of premium paid back as a reward.
source: Niva Bupa / Star Health / Aditya Birla Health Product Features 2024

definition: Health insurance coverage for outpatient department (OPD) consultations and telemedicine (online doctor consultations). Traditionally, health insurance only covered in-patient (IPD) hospitalization. IRDAI now permits OPD cover as a policy feature. Some insurers provide unlimited telemedicine consultations or a fixed number of in-person OPD visits per year. Particularly valuable post-COVID with the rise of digital health platforms.
example: Star Health's Comprehensive Plan includes OPD cover of ₹10,000/year — covering doctor consultations, diagnostics, and pharmacy bills without hospitalization. The policyholder can claim doctor visit bills up to ₹10,000 in a year under OPD cover.
source: IRDAI OPD Product Guidelines / Star Health Product Features

definition: A term insurance policy that can be renewed at the end of each term without requiring fresh medical evidence (guaranteed renewability), though premiums increase with age. Not common in India — most Indian term plans are fixed long-term contracts (20–40 years). Annual renewable term (ART) is common in group term policies. The right to renew without medical underwriting is the key feature.
example: An annual group term life policy at a company renews every year — each year, the premium is repriced based on the group's demographics and claims experience. Individual members are not required to submit fresh medical reports as long as they remain in the group.

definition: India's flagship crop insurance scheme for farmers, managed by the government with IRDAI oversight. Covers yield losses from natural calamities (drought, flood, pest attack, unseasonal rain). Premium is highly subsidized — farmers pay 1.5–5% of sum insured, government pays 40–80% as subsidy. Sum insured is based on the area-based threshold yield for the crop and season. Implemented by empaneled general insurers.
example: A farmer in Maharashtra grows cotton on 2 acres. PMFBY coverage for kharif cotton: sum insured ₹40,000/acre × 2 acres = ₹80,000. Farmer pays 5% premium = ₹4,000. Government pays the balance (~₹36,000 in subsidies). If the crop fails due to drought, the insurer pays up to ₹80,000.

definition: A liability insurance policy protecting company directors and officers from personal financial losses due to wrongful acts in managing the company — including alleged breach of duty, negligence, misstatement, or employment disputes. The policy covers legal defense costs, settlements, and judgments. Required for companies with public shareholders or large borrowings. Increasingly common for Indian startups with institutional investors.
example: A startup's CFO is sued by minority shareholders for financial mismanagement. The D&O policy covers the CFO's legal defense costs (₹50 lakh in lawyer fees) and any settlement amount — protecting personal assets from being at risk for actions taken in official capacity.

definition: A colloquial/popular term for hospitalization health insurance in India — derived from the original 'Mediclaim' policy launched by GIC (General Insurance Corporation) in 1986, which was India's first standardized health insurance product. Today, 'mediclaim' is used generically to refer to any health insurance policy. Formally, modern health insurance is governed by IRDAI health insurance regulations. The original Mediclaim policy is still available through public sector insurers.
example: When a colleague asks 'Do you have mediclaim?' — they are asking if you have health insurance. This term is used interchangeably with health insurance in everyday Indian conversation, though technically 'mediclaim' referred to the original GIC product.

definition: A financial product (sold by life insurers) that provides a regular income stream (monthly, quarterly, or annually) in exchange for a lump sum investment — primarily used for retirement income. Annuity types in India: immediate annuity (income starts immediately after paying lump sum), deferred annuity (income starts after a deferral period), life annuity (income for life, stops at death), joint life annuity (income for policyholder and spouse), annuity with return of purchase price (heirs get back the principal on death). Regulated by IRDAI.
example: At retirement, Suresh invests ₹50 lakh into an immediate life annuity with HDFC Life at 6.5% annuity rate — he receives ₹27,083/month (₹3.25 lakh/year) for the rest of his life. On death, if 'return of purchase price' is selected, his wife gets back ₹50 lakh.

definition: India's voluntary pension scheme regulated by PFRDA (Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority) — distinct from insurance but often compared. NPS has tax benefits under Section 80CCD(1) (₹1.5 lakh within 80C), 80CCD(1B) (additional ₹50,000 deduction), and 80CCD(2) (employer contribution deduction). At retirement (age 60), 60% of NPS corpus can be withdrawn tax-free; 40% must be used to buy an annuity. NPS is not insurance — it is a pension product.
example: A 35-year-old invests ₹12,000/month in NPS till age 60 (25 years). Assuming 10% returns, corpus ≈ ₹1.6 crore at 60. She can withdraw ₹96 lakh tax-free; must invest ₹64 lakh in annuity for monthly pension. Extra: 80CCD(1B) gives ₹50,000 additional deduction beyond 80C.

definition: Income Tax Act Section 80D allows deduction on health insurance premiums paid. Limits for FY2024-25: Self + family (below 60): ₹25,000; Self + family + parents (parents below 60): ₹50,000 (₹25k + ₹25k); Self + family + parents (parents above 60): ₹75,000 (₹25k + ₹50k); Both self and parents above 60: ₹1,00,000. Includes preventive health checkup up to ₹5,000 within the limit. Valid only under old tax regime. Under new tax regime, Section 80D deduction is NOT available.
example: Vikram (35, pays own family's premium ₹22,000) + parents above 60 (pays ₹35,000): total premium ₹57,000. Deduction available: ₹25,000 (self/family) + ₹35,000 (senior parent premium, up to ₹50k limit) = ₹60,000 deduction under 80D. In 30% tax bracket: saves ₹18,000 in taxes.

definition: Mandatory motor insurance under the Motor Vehicles Act 1988 that covers damage or injury caused to third parties (other people, vehicles, or property) by the policyholder's vehicle. Third party (TP) premium rates are fixed by IRDAI annually. TP cover does NOT cover the policyholder's own vehicle damage. Driving without TP insurance is a punishable offense under IPC. TP liability for death/disability is unlimited under law.
example: A car driver jumps a signal and hits a pedestrian causing injury. The car's mandatory TP insurance covers: the pedestrian's medical bills, compensation for disability or death (as awarded by MACT). The car owner's own vehicle damage is NOT covered by TP — they need OD cover for that.
source: Motor Vehicles Act 1988 / IRDAI Motor TP Premium Circular

definition: National Automated Clearing House (NACH) mandate is a bank auto-debit authorization that allows insurers to automatically debit premium amounts from the policyholder's bank account on the due date. Eliminates risk of policy lapse due to forgotten premium payments. Used by most insurers for annual, semi-annual, quarterly, and monthly premium modes. NACH mandate is set up once at policy inception.
example: A term plan policyholder sets up a NACH mandate for ₹15,000/year. Every year on the policy anniversary date, the insurer automatically debits ₹15,000 from the policyholder's savings account. No manual action required — the policy stays active.

definition: A nominee is the person designated by the policyholder to receive insurance proceeds on death — the nominee is the primary recipient. A legal heir is a person entitled to inherit property under succession laws (e.g., Hindu Succession Act). Under IRDAI 2015 rules, if the nominee is a 'beneficial nominee' (spouse, children, parents), they are the absolute owners of the proceeds — not just custodians for legal heirs. If the nominee is a stranger (not family), legal heirs may contest.
example: Arun nominates his wife Sunita in his term policy. On Arun's death, Sunita is the beneficial nominee — she receives and keeps the ₹1 crore benefit as absolute owner. Arun's parents (legal heirs) cannot claim the proceeds from Sunita. If Arun had nominated a friend, the friend must pay the amount to legal heirs (holding it in trust).
source: Insurance Act 1938, Section 39 / IRDAI 2015 Amendment on Beneficial Nominees

definition: Two fundamental types of insurance payout structures. Indemnity policy pays actual financial loss — you can claim only what you actually spent/lost, and not more than that (prevents profit from insurance). Examples: health reimbursement, motor OD, property insurance. Benefit policy pays a predetermined fixed amount regardless of actual loss. Examples: term life insurance, critical illness policy, personal accident policy, hospital cash. Knowing the type matters for claim strategy.
example: Health reimbursement policy = indemnity: hospital charges ₹3 lakh, you get ₹3 lakh. Term plan = benefit: policy is ₹1 crore, family gets ₹1 crore (regardless of actual financial 'loss'). CI policy = benefit: ₹25 lakh paid on diagnosis of cancer, regardless of treatment costs.

definition: A written amendment or addition to an existing insurance policy that modifies its terms, coverage, or details. Changes that can be made via endorsement: nominee change, address update, adding/removing family members (floater), vehicle engine replacement (motor), upgrading sum insured. Endorsements become part of the policy document and must be requested through the insurer's official channel.
example: After a car engine is replaced (due to flood damage), the policyholder must get an IMT endorsement on the motor policy to record the new engine number. Failing to do this can cause claim rejection (mismatched engine number in RC vs policy).

definition: A network of garages (workshops) empaneled with a motor insurer where policyholders can get their vehicles repaired on a cashless basis — the insurer pays the garage directly. The policyholder pays only the deductible and non-covered amounts. Network garages are quality-vetted by the insurer. Repairs at non-network garages require advance payment by the policyholder and later reimbursement from the insurer.
example: HDFC ERGO has a network of 6,800+ cashless garages. If a policyholder's car needs repair after an accident, they take it to a network garage — the garage and HDFC ERGO settle the bill directly (less depreciation and deductible). The policyholder pays only their share (e.g., ₹1,000 compulsory deductible).

definition: A fixed amount that the policyholder must pay out-of-pocket in every motor own damage claim, regardless of claim size. Set by IRDAI: ₹1,000 for private cars (not exceeding 1500cc), ₹2,000 for cars above 1500cc, ₹100 for two-wheelers. This is in addition to any voluntary deductible the policyholder has opted for. The compulsory deductible cannot be waived or covered by any add-on.
example: A car's own damage claim is ₹25,000. Compulsory deductible: ₹1,000 (as per IRDAI for a car below 1500cc). Insurer pays ₹24,000. If the policyholder had also opted for a voluntary deductible of ₹5,000 to reduce premium, insurer pays only ₹19,000.

definition: An optional additional deductible that a motor policyholder chooses to bear in exchange for a premium discount. The higher the voluntary deductible, the larger the premium discount. Options range from ₹2,500 to ₹15,000. Policyholder must evaluate: if they are claim-prone, a high voluntary deductible is expensive; if they rarely claim, the premium saving outweighs the deductible.
example: Opting for ₹5,000 voluntary deductible on a motor policy gives 20% premium discount. If the annual OD premium was ₹8,000, the discount saves ₹1,600/year. If the policyholder makes 2 small claims of ₹6,000 each year, the voluntary deductible costs ₹10,000 (2 × ₹5,000) — much more than ₹1,600 saved.

definition: Insurance covering the building structure and/or its contents against perils such as fire, lightning, explosion, earthquake, flood, burglary, and accidental damage. Two types: Building-only (for homeowners), Contents-only (for renters), or combined. Basis of coverage: reinstatement value or market value. Premium for a ₹50 lakh home structure is approximately ₹2,000–₹5,000/year. Largely underutilized in India despite high awareness of natural calamity risk.
example: A ₹80 lakh apartment (building value ₹50 lakh + contents ₹15 lakh) is insured under a home insurance policy. During monsoon, the apartment floods and ₹8 lakh worth of contents are damaged. The contents section of the home insurance policy pays ₹8 lakh minus deductible.
